framework: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
revisionStatus: A revision to the AI RMF is in progress as of 2026-06-24; version 1.0 (NIST AI 100-1) remains the published framework in force and is what this mapping cites.
scope: Maps the GRADE-Stack reference stack's technical capabilities to the 19 categories of the four AI RMF functions. Framed for a procurement reviewer evaluating what the stack supplies versus what the deploying organization must still own.
sharedResponsibility: The AI RMF is an organizational risk-management framework; a software stack is one input to it, not a substitute for it. `deployer-owned` therefore marks a genuinely organizational control the stack cannot provide — it is a responsibility boundary, NOT a deficiency in the stack. The honest procurement question this mapping answers is: which RMF outcomes does adopting this stack help evidence, and which remain the deployer's program work?

statusLegend.supported: The stack provides a concrete, shipped technical capability or evidence artifact that directly serves this category; the mechanism is named.
statusLegend.partial: The stack supplies meaningful inputs to this category, but the outcome is substantially organizational/process work the deployer completes.
statusLegend.deployer-owned: Fundamentally an organizational control the stack cannot provide. Any stack input is noted, but the obligation is the deploying organization's.

items.title: Policies, processes, procedures and practices for mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks are in place, transparent, and implemented effectively.

mechanisms.name: Production-Readiness Assessment runbook + enforcement-mechanism register
mechanisms.note: Reusable inputs a deployer's AI-risk policy can adopt or reference; not a substitute for the policy itself.

items.note: Deployer-owned: writing and enforcing organizational AI-risk policy is the deploying organization's responsibility. The stack offers a worked example of 'mechanisms, not prose' enforcement to borrow from.

items.title: Accountability structures are in place so the appropriate teams and individuals are empowered, responsible, and trained for mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks.
items.note: Deployer-owned: roles, accountability, and training are organizational structures a software stack cannot supply.

items.title: Workforce diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility processes are prioritized in mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks throughout the lifecycle.
items.note: Deployer-owned and out of a technical stack's scope entirely. Flagged honestly rather than claimed — a procurement reviewer should expect no stack to address this.

items.title: Organizational teams are committed to a culture that considers and communicates AI risk.

mechanisms.name: AI Reliability Scorecard (board-legible, evidence-backed)
mechanisms.note: Directly serves the 'communicates AI risk' half — a one-page readout an executive can act on, every rating traced to evidence.

items.note: Partial: the stack supplies the communication artifact; building the culture that uses it is the deployer's.

items.title: Processes are in place for robust engagement with relevant AI actors.
items.note: Deployer-owned: stakeholder engagement processes are organizational. The open mappings (OWASP, NIST, EU AI Act) are publishable inputs to such engagement, but the process is the deployer's.

items.title: Policies and procedures are in place to address AI risks and benefits arising from third-party software and data and other supply-chain issues.

mechanisms.name: Pinned dependencies + CI secret scan + OWASP ASI04 supply-chain mapping + NOTICE/Apache-2.0 propagation
mechanisms.note: Concrete technical supply-chain controls and a documented third-party-risk mapping.

items.note: Partial: the stack provides the technical controls and a risk mapping; the governing policy around them is the deployer's.

items.title: Context is established and understood.

mechanisms.name: Scoped reference-agent task + Planner/Executor/Validator blueprint + assessment runbook
mechanisms.note: Documents the system's intended use, boundaries, and assessment context as a reusable template.

items.note: Partial: the stack establishes its own context explicitly; a deployer must establish theirs (use case, users, deployment setting).

items.title: Categorization of the AI system is performed.

mechanisms.name: Planner/Executor/Validator blueprint + provider abstraction
mechanisms.note: The architecture, task type, and model categorization are documented as an explicit, reusable categorization of the system.

items.note: Supported: the stack documents its own categorization as a blueprint a deployer can reuse.

items.title: AI capabilities, targeted usage, goals, and expected benefits and costs compared with appropriate benchmarks are understood.

mechanisms.name: Eval suite (≥12 benchmark cases) + cost-per-success metric
mechanisms.note: Capabilities are benchmarked against a fixed case suite, and benefits/costs are quantified per successful outcome on both providers.

items.note: Supported: capability and cost are measured against benchmarks, not asserted.

items.title: Risks and benefits are mapped for all components of the AI system including third-party software and data.

mechanisms.name: OWASP Agentic Top 10 mapping + eval suite OOD/refusal cases
mechanisms.note: Agent-specific risks (incl. supply chain, ASI04) are mapped to mechanisms or flagged as gaps; the eval suite exercises out-of-distribution and refusal cases.

items.note: Supported: a concrete, enforced risk mapping exists (Phase 3A) and is cited here.

items.title: Impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society are characterized.

mechanisms.name: Gateway PII handling (input control)
mechanisms.note: Output PII redaction is one input to impact mitigation, but does not characterize societal impact.

items.note: Deployer-owned: characterizing impacts on people and society is context-specific organizational analysis the stack cannot perform.

items.title: Appropriate methods and metrics are identified and applied.

mechanisms.name: promptfoo eval harness + tolerance-banded reproducibility + cost-per-success + trace coverage
mechanisms.note: Concrete, applied measurement methods with documented metrics and a reproducibility tolerance band.

items.note: Supported: this is the stack's strongest function — measurement is config-as-code and runs in CI.

items.title: AI systems are evaluated for trustworthy characteristics.

mechanisms.name: AI Reliability Scorecard dimensions + portable LLM-as-judge metric
mechanisms.note: Evaluates valid-and-reliable (reliability), secure-and-resilient (guardrail coverage), and accountable-and-transparent (observability) characteristics with evidence.

items.note: Supported for reliability/security/observability characteristics. Honest gap: fairness/bias and privacy-enhanced characteristics are only partially exercised (gateway PII redaction) — a deployer with fairness obligations must add targeted measurement.

items.title: Mechanisms for tracking identified AI risks over time are in place.

mechanisms.name: Eval-gate vs committed baseline + permanent degraded canary + OTel traces
mechanisms.note: Risk is tracked across every commit against a baseline within a tolerance band; traces make each run inspectable over time.

items.note: Supported: continuous, automated tracking is built in, not a periodic manual review.

items.title: Feedback about efficacy of measurement is gathered and assessed.

mechanisms.name: Per-case flakiness / stability reporting + tolerance band
mechanisms.note: Surfaces whether the measurement itself is stable (per-case stability, aggregate tolerance band), a feedback signal on measurement efficacy.

items.note: Partial: the stack reports measurement-stability signals; assessing them and closing the loop organizationally is the deployer's.

items.title: AI risks based on assessments and analytical output from MAP and MEASURE are prioritized, responded to, and managed.

mechanisms.name: Gateway server-side guardrails + eval-gate (blocks regressions) + enforced --max-turns bound
mechanisms.note: Identified risks are responded to by hard mechanisms: guardrails block at the gateway, the eval-gate fails the build on regression, runaway loops are bounded.

items.note: Supported: response to risk is mechanized, not advisory.

items.title: Strategies to maximize AI benefits and minimize negative impacts are planned, prepared, implemented, documented, and informed by relevant AI actors.

mechanisms.name: Gateway guardrails + degraded-mode canary + sovereign/air-gapped variant
mechanisms.note: Implements concrete impact-minimization controls (PII redaction, injection denial, an on-prem option).

items.note: Partial: the stack implements impact-minimization controls; the strategy and stakeholder input that direct them are the deployer's.

items.title: AI risks and benefits from third-party entities are managed.

mechanisms.name: Pinned lockfile + CI secret scan + OWASP ASI04 supply-chain mapping
mechanisms.note: Technical management of third-party/supply-chain risk; pairs with the GOVERN 6 policy boundary.

items.note: Partial: technical third-party controls are present; vendor due-diligence and contracts are the deployer's.

items.title: Risk treatments, including response and recovery and communication plans, for identified and measured AI risks are documented and monitored regularly.

mechanisms.name: OTel tracing (monitoring) + AI Reliability Scorecard (documentation) + eval-gate CI (continuous monitoring) + enforcement-mechanism register
mechanisms.note: Risk treatments are documented (register + scorecard) and monitored continuously (CI gate + traces) rather than reviewed ad hoc.

items.note: Supported for documentation and monitoring of treatments; recovery/communication plans for a specific deployment remain the deployer's to formalize.
